concepts.name: Discovery of Electron – Cathode Rays
concepts.explanation: When high voltage is applied across electrodes in a partially evacuated tube, a stream of negatively charged particles (cathode rays) move from cathode to anode. These rays are electrons, and their properties are independent of the gas used.
concepts.formula_reaction: Cathode rays: negatively charged particles; e⁻

concepts.name: Charge to Mass Ratio (e/mₑ) of Electron
concepts.explanation: J.J. Thomson measured the deflection of cathode rays in perpendicular electric and magnetic fields to determine the charge-to-mass ratio of the electron. The value is e/mₑ = 1.758820 × 10¹¹ C kg⁻¹.

concepts.name: Millikan's Oil Drop Experiment – Charge on Electron
concepts.explanation: Millikan determined the charge on an electron by observing the motion of charged oil droplets in an electric field. The charge is quantized: q = n e, with e = 1.602176 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.

concepts.name: Discovery of Proton and Neutron
concepts.explanation: Proton (positive) was discovered through canal rays, with charge equal and opposite to electron, mass ~1.6726×10⁻²⁷ kg. Neutron (neutral, mass ~1.6749×10⁻²⁷ kg) was discovered by Chadwick via α-particle bombardment of beryllium.

concepts.name: Thomson's Model of Atom (Plum Pudding Model)
concepts.explanation: Thomson proposed that an atom is a sphere of uniform positive charge with electrons embedded in it like seeds in a watermelon. The positive charge and mass are uniformly distributed.

concepts.name: Rutherford's α-particle Scattering Experiment
concepts.explanation: Rutherford bombarded a thin gold foil with α-particles. Most passed through undeflected, some deflected at small angles, and very few bounced back. This led to the nuclear model.

concepts.name: Rutherford's Nuclear Model
concepts.explanation: Atom consists of a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus (radius ~10⁻¹⁵ m) surrounded by electrons revolving in circular orbits, held by electrostatic attraction. Most of the atom is empty space.

concepts.name: Atomic Number (Z) and Mass Number (A)
concepts.explanation: Atomic number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus (equals electrons in a neutral atom). Mass number (A) is the total number of protons and neutrons (nucleons).
concepts.formula_reaction: Z = number of protons; A = Z + number of neutrons

concepts.name: Isotopes and Isobars
concepts.explanation: Isotopes are atoms with same Z but different A (different number of neutrons). Isobars are atoms with same A but different Z. Example: ¹⁴₆C and ¹⁴₇N are isobars.

concepts.name: Drawbacks of Rutherford Model
concepts.explanation: According to classical electromagnetic theory, accelerating electrons in circular orbits should emit radiation, lose energy, and spiral into the nucleus. Rutherford model cannot explain the stability of atoms.

concepts.name: Electromagnetic Radiation – Wave Nature
concepts.explanation: Light and other radiations consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation. They travel at speed c = 3×10⁸ m/s in vacuum.

concepts.name: Planck's Quantum Theory
concepts.explanation: Energy is emitted or absorbed in discrete packets called quanta. Energy of a quantum is proportional to frequency: E = hν, where h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J s. This explains black-body radiation.

concepts.name: Photoelectric Effect
concepts.explanation: When light of sufficient frequency (threshold frequency) strikes a metal surface, electrons are ejected. The kinetic energy of ejected electrons is given by Einstein's equation: K.E. = hν - hν₀, where hν₀ is the work function.

concepts.name: Dual Nature of Electromagnetic Radiation
concepts.explanation: Light exhibits both wave-like (interference, diffraction) and particle-like (photoelectric effect) properties. This dual nature is fundamental.

concepts.name: Atomic Spectra – Emission and Absorption
concepts.explanation: Excited atoms emit radiation at discrete wavelengths, producing line spectra (emission). When white light passes through a sample, certain wavelengths are absorbed, producing dark lines in the continuous spectrum (absorption).

concepts.name: Rydberg Equation for Hydrogen Spectrum
concepts.explanation: The wavenumber of spectral lines is given by ῡ = R_H (1/n₁² - 1/n₂²), where R_H = 1.09677×10⁷ m⁻¹. This equation accounts for all series (Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, etc.) by varying n₁ and n₂.

concepts.name: Lyman Series
concepts.explanation: Transitions from n ≥ 2 to n = 1. Lies in the ultraviolet region.

concepts.name: Balmer Series
concepts.explanation: Transitions from n ≥ 3 to n = 2. Lies in the visible region.

concepts.name: Paschen Series
concepts.explanation: Transitions from n ≥ 4 to n = 3. Lies in the infrared region.

concepts.name: Brackett Series
concepts.explanation: Transitions from n ≥ 5 to n = 4. Lies in the infrared region.

concepts.name: Pfund Series
concepts.explanation: Transitions from n ≥ 6 to n = 5. Lies in the infrared region.

concepts.name: Bohr's Model of Hydrogen Atom
concepts.explanation: Bohr postulated quantized orbits: angular momentum = n(h/2π), energy = -R_H (1/n²). Radius: rₙ = n² a₀ (a₀ = 52.9 pm). Energy levels are quantized and negative, with n=1 being the ground state.

concepts.name: Explanation of Hydrogen Spectrum by Bohr
concepts.explanation: Transitions between quantized energy levels produce photons of energy ΔE = R_H (1/nᵢ² - 1/n_f²). This matches the Rydberg formula and explains the line spectra.

concepts.name: Limitations of Bohr's Model
concepts.explanation: Bohr model fails for multi-electron atoms, cannot explain fine structure (Zeeman effect, Stark effect), and ignores the wave nature of electrons and Heisenberg uncertainty.

concepts.name: de Broglie's Relation – Wave Nature of Matter
concepts.explanation: Matter exhibits wave-like properties; wavelength λ = h / (mv) = h/p. For macroscopic objects, λ is negligible; for subatomic particles, it is detectable and leads to electron diffraction.

concepts.name: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
concepts.explanation: It is impossible to simultaneously determine the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of a particle. Δx · Δp ≥ h/(4π). This rules out precise trajectories for electrons.

concepts.name: Quantum Mechanical Model – Schrödinger Equation
concepts.explanation: The Schrödinger equation (Ĥψ = Eψ) describes the wave function ψ of an electron. Solving it gives quantized energy levels and orbitals (wave functions) for hydrogen-like atoms.

concepts.name: Principal Quantum Number (n)
concepts.explanation: Positive integer (1,2,3…). Determines the shell, size of the orbital, and to a large extent its energy. For hydrogen, energy depends only on n.

concepts.name: Azimuthal (Orbital Angular Momentum) Quantum Number (l)
concepts.explanation: Values: 0 to n-1. Defines the subshell (s, p, d, f for l=0,1,2,3 respectively) and the shape of the orbital. Also influences energy in multi-electron atoms.

concepts.name: Magnetic Quantum Number (m_l)
concepts.explanation: Values: -l to +l. Specifies the orientation of the orbital in space. For a given l, there are (2l+1) orbitals.

concepts.name: Spin Quantum Number (m_s)
concepts.explanation: Values: +½ or -½. Represents the two possible spin orientations of an electron. Two electrons in the same orbital must have opposite spins (Pauli exclusion).

concepts.name: Shapes of Atomic Orbitals
concepts.explanation: s orbitals are spherical; p orbitals are dumbbell-shaped (three orientations: p_x, p_y, p_z); d orbitals have more complex shapes (five orientations). The probability density (|ψ|²) gives the electron cloud.

concepts.name: Energies of Orbitals – (n+l) Rule
concepts.explanation: For multi-electron atoms, the energy of an orbital depends on both n and l. Lower (n+l) means lower energy. If (n+l) is equal, lower n has lower energy. This determines the order of filling (Aufbau).

concepts.name: Aufbau Principle
concepts.explanation: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy (as per (n+l) rule). The filling order can be remembered using the diagonal rule diagram.

concepts.name: Pauli Exclusion Principle
concepts.explanation: No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers. Thus, an orbital can hold at most two electrons with opposite spins.

concepts.name: Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity
concepts.explanation: Electrons fill degenerate orbitals (same energy) singly with parallel spins before pairing up. This minimizes electron repulsion and maximizes exchange energy.

concepts.name: Electronic Configuration of Atoms
concepts.explanation: The distribution of electrons among orbitals is written using notation like 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ ... or using orbital box diagrams. The configuration follows Aufbau, Pauli, and Hund's rules.

concepts.name: Stability of Half-filled and Fully-filled Subshells
concepts.explanation: Configurations like d⁵, d¹⁰, f⁷, f¹⁴ have extra stability due to symmetrical distribution and higher exchange energy. This explains exceptions like Cr ([Ar]3d⁵4s¹) and Cu ([Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹).

concepts.name: Core Electrons and Valence Electrons
concepts.explanation: Core electrons are in filled inner shells (e.g., [Ne] for sodium). Valence electrons are in the outermost shell (highest n) and determine chemical properties.
